coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and characterization of highly emission multicolored carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Mahmoud, Waleed E.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel recipe to prepare highly luminescent and multicolored carbon QDs with narrow bandwidth emission (≈25 nm) and a quantum yield of 81%. The multicolored carbon QDs were prepared via the thermal dehydration of a mixture composed of ascorbic acid and 1,3,5-trihydroxybenzene at various time intervals. The structural phase and optical characterizations divulge that the crystalline perfection and molecular purity of the carbon QDs are the essential factors for the high color purity. The developed synthetic recipe gives rise to the decrease of electron-phonon coupling and improving the free-excitonic emission. The proposed synthetic recipe will open a new avenue for developing next-generation high-performance carbon QDs based light-emitting diodes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Structure
XPS
Luminescence
Quantum confinement

Therefore, the authors gratefully acknowledge technical and financial support from the Ministry of Education and King Abdulaziz University , Jeddah, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The XRD of carbon QDs at various sizes.
TEM images of carbon QDs at various sizes (a) C2 = 1.9 nm, (b) C4 = 2.8 nm, (c) C6 = 3.8 nm, and (d) C8 = 4.7 nm, (inset HRTEM image).
Raman spectroscopy of carbon QDs at various sizes.
High resolution XPS of C1s of carbon QDs at various sizes (a) C2 = 1.9 nm, (b) C4 = 2.8 nm, (c) C6 = 3.8 nm, and (d) C8 = 4.7 nm.
(a) The optical absorption spectra and (b) Tauc's plot of carbon QDs at various sizes.
(a) Photoluminescence spectra and (b) time-resolved luminescence spectra of carbon QDs at various sizes.
The plot of Integrated PL intensity vs. Absorbace for the standard and prepared CQDs samples.
The lattice parameters and C/O ratio of the prepared carbon QDs.
The optical and photoluminescence parameters of the prepared carbon QDs.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and characterization of highly emission multicolored carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: King Abdulaziz University, Faculty of Science, Physics Department, Jeddah, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: King Abdulaziz University, Faculty of Science, Physics Department, Jeddah, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Suez Canal University, Faculty of Science, Physics Department, Ismailia, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Suez Canal University, Faculty of Science, Physics Department, Ismailia, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel recipe to prepare highly luminescent and multicolored carbon QDs with narrow bandwidth emission (≈25 nm) and a quantum yield of 81%. The multicolored carbon QDs were prepared via the thermal dehydration of a mixture composed of ascorbic acid and 1,3,5-trihydroxybenzene at various time intervals. The structural phase and optical characterizations divulge that the crystalline perfection and molecular purity of the carbon QDs are the essential factors for the high color purity. The developed synthetic recipe gives rise to the decrease of electron-phonon coupling and improving the free-excitonic emission. The proposed synthetic recipe will open a new avenue for developing next-generation high-performance carbon QDs based light-emitting diodes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The evolving of light from carbon nanoparticles was a real challenge for scientific community. The development of proficient light-emitting displays based on carbon will not only elevate the potential of the next-generation technological frontiers of carbon optoelectronics and photonics but also provide an efficient alternative to conventional nanocrystals such as PbSe and CdSe quantum dots in light-emitting applications [1]. The discovery of light emission from carbon nanoparticles with mean size less than 10 nm elicited rigorous researches on light-emitting carbon nanocrystals to accomplish their inclusive potential applications [2]. Recently, various efforts have been devoted to synthesize highly luminescent carbon QDs with a quantum yield of around 73% such as surface passivation, hetero-atom doping, and separation of carbon from organic compounds [3,4]. Cailotto et al. [5] mentioned the synthesis of CQDs from sugar and ascorbic acid. The results showed that the synthetic procedure yields luminescent of 28%. Jia et al. [6] reported a novel one-pot fabrication of CQDs facilely by low-temperature aqueous heating. In a typical synthesis ascorbic acid was dissolved in water, followed by adding copper acetates. The mixture was stirred for 10 min at room temperature and then maintained at 90 °C under stirring for 5 h. The reaction mixture slowly turned from an orange suspension to a clear golden yellow solution, indicating the formation of CQDs. The results revealed that the produced CQDs did not show a high PL intensity. Sun et al. provided the 19.2% QY of CQDs obtained by the hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and 2-(2-aminoethoxy)-ethanol [7]. Du et al. reported that fluorescence CQDs with 43.8% QY for sensitive and selective detection of iodide [8]. Nevertheless, despite the rigorous studies on the optical and electronic properties of carbon QDs, it gives broad bandwidth emission with full width at half maximum exceeding 80 nm [9]. Undeniably, such a broad emission hinders the application of carbon QDs-based LEDs in high color purity displays. Here, we report the synthesis of multicolored, narrow bandwidth (FWHM of 25 nm), and high emission color-purity carbon QDs with a quantum yield that reaches 81%. The synthesis was performed by carefully selecting two organic compounds (ascorbic/1,3,5-trihydroxybenzene) as the reagents. The ascorbic acid was employed as carbon source material and the 1,3,5-trihydroxybenzene was used an efficient surface passivation agent. The structural phase and the luminescence characteristics have been studied. The developed synthetic recipe leads to the decrease of electron-phonon coupling and the increase of free-excitonic emission.

The multicolored carbon quantum dots were prepared via the thermal dehydration approach as follows. The reaction starts with the dissolve of ascorbic acid (1 g) and 1,3,5-trihydroxybenzene (2 g) in deionized water (100 ml). This mixture was stirred at a fixed temperature of 85 °C for 30 min. Then hydrochloric acid (35 ml) was added dropwise to this mixture until the color of the solution turned from colorless to pale yellow. The reaction kept refluxing for 1 h to increase the size of the formed nanoparticles. During the reaction process, the samples were withdrawn each 15 min to get carbon QDs with different sizes. The obtained colloidal solutions with various colors were quenched in an ice bath. The structural phase of the samples was studied using X-ray diffraction (RIGAKU-ULTIMA). The XPS measurements were carried out using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (PHI5000-VersaprobII-ULVAC-PHI. inc). The TEM measurements were performed using Transmission electron microscopy (JEM-ARM200F). The optical properties were studied using UV–vis spectrophotometer (JASCO-V770) and the fluorescence (PerkinElmer-S55).

The crystal structure of the as-synthesized multicolored carbon QDs was studied using X-ray diffraction (XRD). Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of the as-prepared carbon QDs at various time intervals. The XRD spectrum shows a single broad (002) peak centered at around 25.4°. The intensity of this peak increases with increasing the time of reaction, implying the increase of the crystallite size. The average crystallite size was determined using Scherrer's formula [10,11].
(1)
R =
k λ
β
cos
θ

The increase of the time of reaction leads to the increase of the crystallite size from 1.4 nm to 4.8 nm as indicated in Table 1 . However, the XRD peaks show a slight shift toward the lower angles, suggesting the increase of the d-space parameter. Therefore, the inter-planar d-spacing parameter was calculated using Bragg's law [12,13].
(2)
d =
λ
2
sin
θ
Table 1 reveals that the increase of the time of reaction leads to the increase of the d-spacing parameter from 0.211 nm to 0.256 nm.

The morphology of the prepared carbon QDs at various time intervals was investigated using the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 2 ). The TEM images reveal that the formed carbon nanoparticles have spherical shape. These nanoparticles have a narrow size distribution with a mean size in the range from 1.4 ± 0.1 nm to 4.7 ± 0.2 nm (Table). The TEM images show lattice fringes with a spacing in the range 0.21–0.27 nm corresponding to the (100) inter-planar spacing of almost defect-free crystalline structure of the carbon [14,15]. Therefore, the increase of time of reaction of the carbon QDs leads to the increase of the particle size and the d-spacing inter-planar distance, which in agreement with the XRD results. The degree of graphitization of the carbon QDs was investigated from Raman measurements (Fig. 3 ). The Raman spectrum of the prepared carbon QDs showed a peak at 1348 cm−1 which ascribed to graphitic disorder and another peak at 1572 cm−1, which assigned to the G band related to the vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms of the conjugated carbon chains [16–18]. However, it is noticed that the increase of the reaction time results in the decrease of the D-band, implying that the as long as the reaction time increases the rate of thermal decomposition of oxalic acid increases and converts to carbon with low crystal defects. To assess this conclusion, the crystallite size, La, of the sp2 lattice was determined according to Tuinstra-Koeing equation [19–21].
(3)
I D
I G
=
2.4 x
10
− 10
λ 4
L a

Table 2 reveals the increase of crystallite size, La, with increasing the thermal decomposition reaction time confirms the enhancement of the crystallinity of carbon QDs. These results are in agreement with XRD and TEM measurements.

XPS measurements were performed to explore the mechanism of converting the ascorbic acid to carbon QDs through the increase of the time of thermal decomposition. The high resolution XPS of the C1s spectra for the produced carbon QDs during the thermal decomposition of ascorbic acid at various time intervals are displayed in Fig. 4 . The high resolution C1s spectrum of the carbon QDs during thermal decomposition process of ascorbic acid after 15 min (C1) shows four deconvoluted peaks at 285.1 eV, 286.5 eV, 287.3 eV and 289.1 eV which ascribed to the C–C, C–OH, CO and OC–OH groups [22–25]. Moreover, the intensity of the C–OH, CO and OC–OH peaks decreases with the increase of the time of thermal decomposition of ascorbic acid, implying that the increase of reaction time results in the decomposition of the ascorbic acid and the conversion of ascorbic acid to carbon nanoparticles and hydroxyl groups (OH) on the surface of the carbon nanoparticles. To assess this conclusion, the concentration of oxygen, carbon and C/O ratio were determined and presented in Table 2. It is noteworthy that the increase of time of thermal decomposition leads to the increase of carbon yield reaches 73%. To the best of our knowledge, the yield of carbon atoms using the developed synthetic recipe is considered among the largest ever reported to date for carbon QDs [26,27]. Therefore, the proposed mechanis for the convertion of ascorbic acid to carbon QDs may be supposed as follow. The increase of the thermal decomposition time leads to the breaking of the ascorbic acid bonds and the liberation of carbon atoms. The increase of time of growth results in the increase of the size of the carbon particles which surrounded by the 1,3,5-trihydroxybenzene as strong chealting agent which behaves like an efficient surface passivation of the formed CQDs.

Fig. 5 a shows the optical absorption spectra of the prepared carbon QDs with various sizes. A red-shift of the absorption curves as the average size of carbon QDs increasing from 1.4 to 4.7 nm was observed. The bandgap energies were calculated using Tauc's equation [28–30] as depicted in Fig. 5b.
(4)
[ α h v ]
2
=
A
(
h v − E g
)

A decrease from 2.94 to 2.31eV with increasing the carbon QDs size was observed (Table 2), which reveals that the quantum confinement effect dominates the optical and electronic properties of the carbon QDs.

Fig. 6 a shows the luminescence spectra of carbon QDs. These spectra show sharp excitonic emission peaks with narrow bandwidth of 25 ± 4 nm (Table 2), which is much smaller than the previously reported carbon QDs with broadband luminescence spectra (>80 nm) [31]. Likewise, the carbon QDs also reveal small Stokes shifts of 8 ± 2 nm (Table 2), which is much smaller than those of the common carbon QDs (>70 nm), inferring the weak electron–phonon coupling of the prepared carbon QDs. It is also portentous that the emission of carbon QDs arises from band-edge exciton-state decay rather than from defect-state decay, which is dominated by the common carbon QDs [32]. The gradual red-shift of the excitonic emission peak from 492 nm (blue) to 574 nm (dark green) is well correlated with the increase of the mean size from 1.4 to 4.7 nm. To get deep insight into the dynamic of exciton recombination, the time-resolved luminescence spectra were measured as shown in Fig. 6b. This behavior obeys the monoexponential decay, which may be given by the relation [33,34].
(5)
I
( t )
= A
e
−
(
t τ
)
where A is the decay amplitudes, τ is the transition time, respectively. The prepared carbon QDs showed monoexponential decay with luminescence lifetime in the range from 8.2 to 7.3 ns (Table 2). The monoexponential decay indicates that the radiative decay is highly pure with minimal non-radiative contributions, which is helpful for efficient PL emission [35]. The quantum yield was calculated in reference to Cumarin 1 using the relation [36–40].
(6)
Q y
=
Q
s t d
G a r
d
s t d
n 2
G r a
d
s a m p
n
s t d
2
where, Qy and Qstd denote the fluorescence quantum yield of the prepared samples (CQDs) and standard (Cumarin-1) compounds, respectively. The ‘‘Grad’’ denotes the gradient (slop) of the integrated PL intensity vs. absorbance graph (shown in Fig. 7 ) and n denoted the refractive index of the solvent, respectively. Fig. 7 shows the plot of integrated Pl intensity vs. absorbance for the standard (Cumarin-1) and the prepared samples of CQDs. The Grad (slope) of the curves was determined and the fitted lines showed correlation factor R2 = 0.9995. The calculated quantum yields of the prepared CQDs is presented Table 2. It is found that the increase of the CQDs size results in an increase of the quantum yield from 77% to 83%. It is noteworthy that the QY values are among the highest recorded values for carbon QDs to date [41,42]. These unique characteristics may enable the prepared carbon QDs to be used as excellent active materials for high-performance light-emitting displays.

A series of multicolored carbon quantum dots with different sizes were prepared using a thermal dehydration approach for the first time. The prepared quantum dots showed high crystallinity as confirmed from XRD measurements. The TEM measurement revealed that the prepared carbon QDs have high monodispersed nanoparticles with a mean size in the range 1.4–4.7 nm. The HRTEM measurement confirmed the high crystallinity of the prepared carbon QDs. The XPS measurement showed that the carbon yield using the proposed synthetic recipe reaches 73%. The luminescence spectra of carbon QDs show sharp excitonic emission peaks with narrow bandwidth around 25 ± 2 nm, inferring that the emission of carbon QDs originates from band-edge exciton-state decay. The absolute quantum yield was found to be around 81 ± 4%. It is noteworthy that the QY values are among the highest recorded values for carbon QDs to date. The developed synthetic recipe gives rise to the decrease of electron-phonon coupling and improving the free-excitonic emission.
Waleed E. Mahmoud: The sole author of this work and the owner of the idea.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research work was funded by Institutional Fund Projects under grant no (IFPNC-004-130-2020). Therefore, the authors gratefully acknowledge technical and financial support from the Ministry of Education and King Abdulaziz University, Jeddah, Saudi Arabia.
